entities.name: Elioun (Hypsistus)
entities.raw_quote: "Contemporary with these was one Elioun, called Hypsistus (that is, 'the Most High') and his wife 'Beruth"

entities.name: Epigeus (Autocthon, Ouranos)
entities.raw_quote: "By these were begotten Epigeus, or Autocthon, whom they afterwards called Ouranos (Heaven)…

entities.name: Ge (Terra)
entities.raw_quote: "he had a sister of the same parents called Ge (Earth), and by reason of her beauty the earth was called by the same name."

entities.name: Cronus (Saturn, elder)
entities.raw_quote: "the elder Cronus…is the same as Thoth, i.e., Cush."

entities.name: Thoth (Hermes)
entities.raw_quote: "Thoth was the counsellor of Tammuz and of Osiris, and both the latter are the same as the second Cronus, i.e., Nimrod…

entities.name: Nimrod
entities.raw_quote: "Thoth…both the latter are the same as the second Cronus, i.e., Nimrod…

entities.name: Semiramis (Rhea)
entities.raw_quote: "Now 'Anobret,' the wife of Cronus or Cush, must be Semiramis…

entities.name: Tower of Babel
entities.raw_quote: "the building of such a tower as Babel could not have been undertaken while the Patriarch was supported by the bulk of his descendants…

entities.name: human sacrifice
entities.raw_quote: "This, as already pointed out, seems to have been the origin of human sacrifice.

entities.name: Baitulos (stone)
entities.raw_quote: "This stone was called in Grecian mythology 'Baitulos'…

entities.name: Zoroaster (Chaldean)
entities.raw_quote: "The tyrant Zoroaster, of the line of Cham (Ham), was one of the founders of the Tower of Babel; he sacrificed innumerable victims to the daemons;

entities.name: Baal
entities.raw_quote: "This was also the origin of the human sacrifices to Baal and Moloch, to whom children were especially acceptable…

entities.name: Moloch
entities.raw_quote: "This was also the origin of the human sacrifices to Baal and Moloch, to whom children were especially acceptable…

entities.name: Osiris
entities.raw_quote: "the sacrifice of the first-born to the Sun god Osiris, that is, Nimrod, was one of the most notorious of the Egyptian rites.

summary: This chunk presents a theological-historical reconstruction linking ancient Near Eastern mythologies (Sanchoniathon, Greek, Phoenician) to a proto-necromantic tradition rooted in Nephilim ancestry. It identifies Noah as Ouranos (Heaven), Cush/Saturn/Cronus as the rebellious tower-builder and originator of demon worship, and Nimrod as the second Cronus—linking these figures to spirit communication, human sacrifice, and ritual stone veneration. The Tower of Babel is framed as a necromantic attempt to contact demonic powers via 'high places' and occult knowledge. Semiramis (Rhea/Anobret) plays a key role in subverting paternal sacrifice through substitution with the Baitulos stone—a motif tied to later Roman stone worship.
key_passages: "the building of such a tower as Babel could not have been undertaken while the Patriarch was supported by the bulk of his descendants…
"Thoth was the counsellor of Tammuz and of Osiris, and both the latter are the same as the second Cronus, i.e., Nimrod…
"This, as already pointed out, seems to have been the origin of human sacrifice.
"Now 'Anobret,' the wife of Cronus or Cush, must be Semiramis…
"the worship of stones, which were the symbols of so many of the gods.
